title: CSM - Construction Site Management Software
shortDescription: A cross-platform construction site management system built with a desktop app for supervisors and a mobile app for workers, designed to run offline-first in low-connectivity environments.
fullDescription: CSM was my HTL diploma project, built with a team of four. Construction sites still run on physical Post-its — we decided to fix that. We built a centralized, real-time lean management system consisting of a desktop application for site supervisors and a mobile application for on-site workers. The core challenge was reliability: construction sites often have limited or no internet connectivity, so the system had to function offline-first and sync when connectivity was restored. The project was managed end-to-end using an agile Scrum workflow with a Jira board for sprint planning, task tracking and backlog organization — mirroring how real software teams operate.
features: Project-based construction site management
Timeline-based visualization of work steps and pulls (lean workflow)
Management of work steps, obstacles, and changes
Real-time updates via WebSockets
Role-based access and permission system
QR-code-based project access for mobile users
Offline-capable mobile application with automatic sync
Separate desktop and mobile workflows optimized for different user roles

title: Notion Task Scheduler
shortDescription: A Python automation that reads tasks from Notion and schedules them automatically into Google Calendar — respecting priorities, deadlines and existing events.
fullDescription: I came across an ad for Motion — an AI-powered calendar that automatically schedules your tasks. I thought it was a great idea, so I decided to build it myself. The scheduler integrates the Notion API and Google Calendar API to fully automate daily planning. It pulls tasks from two separate Notion databases — one AI-managed, one manual — and schedules them into Google Calendar by finding available time slots while respecting existing events, priorities, deadlines and estimated durations. The core scheduling algorithm sorts tasks by hard deadline, priority and soft deadline, then iterates through existing calendar events to find the earliest conflict-free slot for each task. It handles timezone-aware datetime comparisons, all-day event parsing, and resolves overlaps through iterative slot-finding. Once scheduled, it writes the start and end time back to Notion and updates the task status automatically. The system runs as a background process using APScheduler, polling for Notion changes every few minutes and rescheduling dynamically when tasks are added or modified.
features: Pulls tasks from two Notion databases — AI-managed and manual
Automatically finds conflict-free time slots in Google Calendar
Respects task priority, hard deadlines, soft deadlines and estimated duration
Writes scheduled start and end times back to Notion
Updates task status automatically (Scheduled, Overdue, Done, Skipped)
Handles timezone-aware datetime comparisons
Supports both timed and all-day Google Calendar events
Runs as a background process, polling for changes every few minutes
Dynamically reschedules when tasks are added or modified in Notion
Filters out completed or skipped tasks to avoid unnecessary scheduling

title: Darkknight
shortDescription: A procedurally generated 2D platformer fusing the brutal combat of Dark Souls 3 with the chaotic level design of Spelunky — built entirely in Unity with self-made pixel art.
fullDescription:
Darkknight started as an experiment: what would happen if you combined the tight, punishing combat of Dark Souls 3 with the procedurally generated chaos of Spelunky?

The result is a 2D platformer demo with a complete game loop, focused on difficult combat and responsive mechanics. Every level is generated at runtime using a completely custom procedural generation system that builds a tilemap from scratch. Using a combination of prebuilt room layouts and pure randomness.

The enemy AI features patrolling behaviour, distinct attack patterns per enemy type, and dynamic player tracking for flying enemies like bats that actively chase the player. The visual style is built entirely with self-made pixel art, enhanced by 2D real-time lighting and post-processing effects to give it a unique feel.
features: Custom procedural generation system that builds a tilemap at runtime
Complete game loop with a playable demo
Enemy AI with patrolling behaviour and distinct attack patterns
Dynamic player tracking for flying bat enemies
Self-made pixel art assets
2D real-time lighting and post-processing for atmospheric visuals
Combat system inspired by Dark Souls 3

title: CLI Text Editor
shortDescription: A lightweight command line text editor built in C# and .NET, inspired by Nano.
fullDescription:
I built this because I was fascinated by the VIM editor and wanted to understand how terminal-based editors actually work under the hood — so I made my own.

Inspired by Nano, it runs entirely in the terminal and handles the core editing experience: navigating files, editing text and managing the cursor — all within the constraints of a command line interface.

Building it taught me a lot about how .NET handles console I/O, terminal rendering and low-level input processing.
features: Runs entirely in the terminal
Core text editing and cursor navigation
Inspired by the Nano editor
Built from scratch in C# and .NET

title: This Portfolio
shortDescription: A fast, dark-themed developer portfolio built with Next.js 15, React 19 and Tailwind CSS v4 — fully static, type-safe, and designed from scratch.
fullDescription:
This is the site you're looking at right now.

I built it because I wanted a portfolio that felt like mine — not a template, not a theme, but something designed and engineered top to bottom. The stack is Next.js 15 with React Server Components and the React Compiler, so there's almost no client-side JavaScript: only the nav (scroll state) and the image lightbox ship any JS to the browser.

The design system lives entirely in a single globals.css using Tailwind v4's @theme block — no tailwind.config.js, no plugin configuration. Custom tokens like --color-surface, gradient utilities, and card glow effects are all defined there and consumed directly as Tailwind classes.

Project data is static JSON with a thin TypeScript data-access layer. At build time, Next.js generates a static page for every project slug via generateStaticParams — the whole site exports as plain HTML and deploys to any CDN with zero server costs.

The image gallery supports an arbitrary number of screenshots with a layout that adapts to the count: single full-width, side-by-side pairs, or a featured-first grid. Clicking any image opens a keyboard-navigable lightbox without any third-party library.
features: Fully static output — every page pre-rendered at build time via generateStaticParams
React Server Components throughout; only 2 client components in the entire app
React Compiler enabled — no manual useMemo or useCallback needed
Tailwind v4 design system defined entirely in CSS with @theme tokens
Multi-image gallery with adaptive layout (1 / 2 / 3+ image modes)
Custom lightbox with keyboard navigation (← → Esc) — no third-party dependency
Dark-first design with per-project accent colors and card glow effects
SEO metadata generated per project via generateMetadata

title: LifeOS
shortDescription: A personal productivity dashboard with AI-powered capture, Google Calendar sync, habit tracking, and drag-and-drop scheduling — all running locally in the browser with no backend.
fullDescription:
I built LifeOS because every productivity app I tried became another thing to manage — and because I wanted a project to experiment with Next.js.

It's a personal daily dashboard built with Next.js and TailwindCSS — one screen, one day, no clutter. Habits, time blocks and tasks in a single view

The AI capture page accepts anything — freeform text, photos of handwritten notes, whiteboard shots — and uses Claude's vision API to extract structured tasks and calendar events automatically. You type or photograph, it figures out the rest.

The timeline integrates with Google Calendar via their REST API, rendering your day as a visual timeline. Drag and drop two events and the times update in Google Calendar in real time, with an overlap-detection algorithm handling conflicts automatically. Past events collapse as the day moves forward.

Everything lives in localStorage. No account, no server, no data leaving the browser except directly to Anthropic and Google APIs.
features: AI capture: paste text or upload a photo and Claude extracts tasks and calendar events
Google Calendar sync with real-time PATCH updates when events are dragged and reordered
Overlap-aware scheduling: tasks can be pushed to the next free calendar slot with automatic duration trimming
Habit streak tracking with a rolling date-strip UI
Inline task and event editing — title, deadline, start/end times editable in place
Zero backend: all data in localStorage, static export deployable to GitHub Pages
